title: Transformer Block Components
learning_outcomes: Explain absolute positional encodings and the Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) relative distance rotation.

content_sections.title: Layer Normalization: Pre-LN vs. Post-LN
content_sections.content:
Transformer blocks use Layer Normalization (LN) to stabilize activations. 

*   **Post-LN:** Normalization occurs after the residual addition: $\mathbf{x}_{l+1} = \text{LN}(\mathbf{x}_l + \text{SubLayer}(\mathbf{x}_l))$. While historically common, Post-LN requires linear learning rate warmup schedules to prevent gradient explosions in early layers during initialization.
*   **Pre-LN:** Normalization is applied to the input before the sublayer: $\mathbf{x}_{l+1} = \mathbf{x}_l + \text{SubLayer}(\text{LN}(\mathbf{x}_l))$. Pre-LN maintains a direct identity path from the first layer to the last, stabilizing gradient flows. This allows models to scale to hundreds of layers without warmup tricks.

content_sections.title: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE)
content_sections.content:
Since self-attention is permutation-invariant, models require positional encodings. Modern architectures replace absolute positional encodings with **Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)**.

RoPE projects position information by rotating query and key vectors in complex space. For a 2D vector slice $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2]^{\top}$ at position $m$, the rotation is:

$$\mathbf{R}_{m\theta} \mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos m\theta & -\sin m\theta \\ \sin m\theta & \cos m\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

This rotation preserves relative distance: the dot product between a query at position $m$ and a key at position $n$ depends only on their relative distance $m-n$. This allows the model to generalize to context lengths beyond those seen during training.
